paragraphs.body: My name is Luca Romano, and I am a student at the University of Pittsburgh who came across your essay in my English class and found a deep appreciation for it. I recently read it and thoroughly enjoyed the essay, “Son of Mr.Green Jeans,” which focused on the hardships and expectations of fatherhood through an unconventional structure. The use of fragmented parts and small anecdotes through the essay that seem contrasted but yet are all connected was very unique and was a great way of showing what it means to be a father and going through fatherhood.

I noticed your discussion of your own father and how he had passed away. This was the beginning to a bunch of small anecdotes talking about the struggles of fatherhood, where you must have not been able to look up to a role model which makes it even more challenging. I think the contrasts you use like talking about humans and then other animals care like when you mentioned penguins was very powerful. This exploited that nobody will ever be the perfect parent no matter how simple or complex they are.

Though I haven’t gone through what you have gone through, I did lose my grandfather at a young age. Being able to reflect on it all of these years helps me relate and put into perspective what my Dad must’ve gone through in correlation to some of the things your essay talks about. This essay encouraged me to reflect on my father figure and appreciate his presence in my life. I realize how much it has shaped my identity and how much credit I owe to him.

annotations.text: perspective what my Dad must’ve gone through in correlation to some of the things your essay talks about.
annotations.comment: I'd really love to know more about this--what connections can you make between your own relationships and the text?

paragraphs.body: I am curious about something though. When you thought about making this essay, and wrote the draft in your imagination, was it always fragmented like this? Where the fragments out of order? How did that come to be? Looking at the essay deeper, I think this adds to the theme and power of the writing, I just didn’t know if you knew that back when first drafting this piece.

annotations.text: When you thought about making this essay, and wrote the draft in your imagination, was it always fragmented like this? Where the fragments out of order? How did that come to be? Looking at the essay deeper, I think this adds to the theme and power of the writing, I just didn’t know if you knew that back when first drafting this piece.
annotations.comment: I find it super interesting that you are asking the author about his creative process, rather than focusing on the content of the piece--a very writerly move.

paragraphs.body: Thank you for sharing an essay that was so thought invoking and powerful. This piece has challenged me to think deeper about fatherhood and all of the expectations that a father has, from a personal level to a societal level.

annotations.comment: You're doing a really great job of engaging this work and the author with fragments from your own personal life. I'm looking forward to seeing how you develop these notions and continue to interact with the text.
